title: Read line plots (data with fractions)
description: Practice reading and interpreting data that is graphed on line plots. The data graphed includes fractions.

assessment_items.question_content:
The lengths of some caterpillars are shown below.  

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**How many caterpillar lengths measured $4\dfrac34$ inches?** 
 
[[Ōśā input-number 1]] caterpillars
hints: First, we need to find $4\dfrac34$ inches on the number line.

$4\dfrac34$ is after $4$ but before $5$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $4$ equal parts, or fourths, between each whole. We can use equivalent fractions to label each tick.

[[Ōśā image 2]]

Each dot represents one caterpillar.

So, we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent caterpillars that are $4\dfrac34$ inches long.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $\blueE2$ dots that represent lengths that are $4\dfrac34$ inches.
$\blueD{2}$ caterpillar lengths measured $4\dfrac34$ inches.

options.alt: A line plot labeled Caterpillar lengths (inches) shows, moving left to right, labeled tick marks at 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10. Three unlabeled tick marks appear between each labeled tick mark. Dots are plotted as follows: 2 dots above 4, 4 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 4, 2 dots above the third unlabeled tick mark after 4, 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 5, 2 dots above 6, 3 dots above 7, 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 8, and 2 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 9.

So, we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent jumps that are $3\dfrac34$ feet long.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There is $\greenE1$ dot that represents lengths that are $3\dfrac34$ feet.
$\greenD{1}$ jump measured $3\dfrac34$ feet long.

options.alt: A line plot labeled Frog jump lengths (feet) shows, moving left to right, labeled tick marks at 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7. Three unlabeled tick marks appear between each labeled tick mark. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above 2, 2 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 2, 2 dots above the first unlabeled tick mark after 3, 3 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 3, 1 dot above the third unlabeled tick mark after 3, 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 4, 3 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 4, 2 dots above 5, 2 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 5, and 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 6.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**How many jumps are  between $2\dfrac14$  feet and $4\dfrac34$ feet?** 
 
[[Ōśā input-number 1]] jumps
hints: First, we need to find $2\dfrac14$ and $4\dfrac34$ feet on the number line.

$2\dfrac14$ is after $2$ but before $3$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $4$ equal parts, or fourths, between each whole. We can use equivalent fractions to label each tick.

[[Ōśā image 2]]

$4\dfrac34$ is after $4$ but before $5$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $4$ equal parts, or fourths, between each whole. We can use equivalent fractions to label each tick.

[[Ōśā image 2]]

Each dot represents one frog jump. 

So, we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that are between  $2\dfrac14$  feet and $4\dfrac34$ feet.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $\greenD{12}$ dots that are between $2\dfrac14$  feet and $4\dfrac34$ feet.

$\greenD{12}$ jumps were  between $2\dfrac14$  feet and $4\dfrac34$ feet.

assessment_items.question_content:
The heights of some sunflowers are shown below.  

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**How many more sunflowers are $7$ feet tall than are $5\dfrac12$ feet tall?** 
 
[[Ōśā input-number 1]] sunflowers
hints: First, we need to find $5\dfrac12$ feet on the number line.

$5\dfrac12$ is after $5$ but before $6$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $4$ equal parts, or fourths, between each whole. We can use equivalent fractions to label each tick.

[[Ōśā image 2]]

Each dot represents one sunflower. 

So, we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent sunflowers that are $5\dfrac12$ feet tall.  

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $\greenD2$ dots that represent heights that are $5\dfrac12$ feet tall.

Then we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent sunflowers that are $7$ feet tall.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $\purpleD4$ dots that represent heights that are $7$ feet tall.
To find how many more, we can subtract $\purpleD4 - \greenD2 =\goldE2$
$\goldE2$ more sunflowers are $7$ feet tall than are $5\dfrac12$ feet tall.

options.alt: A line plot labeled sunflower height (feet) shows, moving left to right, labeled tick marks at 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10. Three unlabeled tick marks appear between each labeled tick mark. Dots are plotted as follows: 2 dots above the first unlabeled tick mark after 3, 2 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 3, 3 dots above 4, 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 4, 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 5, 2 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 5, 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 6, 4 dots above 7, 2 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 7, 3 dots above the first unlabeled tick mark after 8, 1 dot above the third unlabeled tick mark after 8, and 2 dots above the first unlabeled tick mark after 9.

assessment_items.question_content:
The jump lengths of some frogs are shown below.  

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**How many fewer jumps were $6\dfrac14$ feet long than were $3\dfrac12$ feet long?** 
 
[[Ōśā input-number 1]] jumps
hints: First, we need to find $3\dfrac12$ feet on the number line.

$3\dfrac12$ is after $3$ but before $4$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $4$ equal parts, or fourths, between each whole. We can use equivalent fractions to label each tick.

[[Ōśā image 2]]

Each dot represents one jump.

So, we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent  jumps that are $3\dfrac12$ feet long.  

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $\maroonD3$ dots that represent jumps that are $3\dfrac12$ feet.

Next, we need to find $6\dfrac14$ feet on the number line.

$6\dfrac14$ is after $6$ but before $7$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $4$ equal parts, or fourths, between each whole. We can use equivalent fractions to label each tick.

[[Ōśā image 2]]

Then we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent jumps that are $6\dfrac14$ feet long.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There is $\purpleD1$ dot that represent jumps that are $6\dfrac14$ feet.
To find how many fewer, we can subtract $\maroonD3 - \purpleD1 =\greenD2$
$\greenD2$ fewer jumps were $6\dfrac14$ feet than were $3\dfrac12$ feet.

options.alt: A line plot labeled Frog jump lengths (feet) shows, moving left to right, labeled tick marks at 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7. Three unlabeled tick marks appear between each labeled tick mark. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above 2, 2 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 2, 2 dots above the first unlabeled tick mark after 3, 3 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 3, 1 dot above the third unlabeled tick mark after 3, 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 4, 3 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 4, 2 dots above 5, 2 dots above the second unlabeled tick mark after 5, and 1 dot above the first unlabeled tick mark after 6.

assessment_items.question_content:
The lengths of some elephant footprints are shown below.  

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**How many more footprints are $46\dfrac12$ cm long than are $48\dfrac12$ centimeters long?** 
 
[[Ōśā input-number 1]] footprints
hints: First, we need to find $46\dfrac12$ centimeters on the number line.

$46\dfrac12$ is after $46$ but before $47$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $2$ equal parts, or halves, between each whole. We can label each tick.


[[Ōśā image 2]]

Each dot represents one footprint. 

So, we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent  footprints that are $46\dfrac12$ centimeters long.  

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There are $\greenD5$ dots that represent footprints that are $46\dfrac12$ centimeters long.

Next, we need to find $48\dfrac12$ centimeters on the number line.

$48\dfrac12$ is after $48$ but before $49$.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

Then we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent footprints that are $48\dfrac12$ centimeters long.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

There is $\purpleD1$ dot that represent footprints that are $48\dfrac12$ centimeters long.
To find how many more, we can subtract $\greenD5 - \purpleD1 =\blueD4$
$\blueD4$ more footprints are $46\dfrac12$ centimeters long than are $48\dfrac12$ centimeters long.

options.alt: A line plot labeled Elephant footprint lengths (centimeters) shows, moving left to right, labeled tick marks at 42, 43, 44, 45, 45, 47, 48, 49, and 50. An unlabeled tick mark appears in the middle between each labeled tick mark. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above 43, 3 dots above the unlabeled tick mark between 44 and 45, 2 dots above 45, 4 dots above the unlabeled tick mark between 45 and 46, 5 dots above the unlabeled tick mark between 46 and 47, 3 dots above 47, 2 dots above 48, 1 dot above the unlabeled tick mark between 48 and 49, and 2 dots above the unlabeled tick mark between 49 and 50.

Next, we need to find $10\dfrac12$ grams on the number line.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

Then we need to count all of the dots on the line plot that represent strawberries that weigh $10\dfrac12$ grams.

$\maroonD3$ fewer strawberries weigh  $7\dfrac12$ grams than weighs $10\dfrac12$ grams.
